steps.title: Apply the product property of radicals
steps.content:
The cube root of a product equals the product of the cube roots:

$$\sqrt[3]{x^3 \cdot y^6} = \sqrt[3]{x^3} \cdot \sqrt[3]{y^6}$$

steps.title: Simplify each cube root
steps.content:
For $\sqrt[3]{x^3}$: since $x^3$ is a perfect cube, $\sqrt[3]{x^3} = x$.

For $\sqrt[3]{y^6}$: rewrite the exponent as a multiple of 3. Since $y^6 = (y^2)^3$, we have $\sqrt[3]{y^6} = \sqrt[3]{(y^2)^3} = y^2$.

steps.title: Multiply the simplified terms
steps.content:
The final expression is:

$$x \cdot y^2$$

That matches choice B — B is correct.
